Subject: Projectile with delay-action fuse
Subject Details: Delay-action fuse is a time-delay fuse: after a shell hits its target, a small part inside breaks a moment later, setting off the main explosive only after the shell has had time to dig in. The idea fed into the delay fuzes now used in armor-piercing and bunker-busting munitions; surviving examples are mostly in technical collections, not museums.

Subject: Aerodrome experiments, status
Subject Details: Langley’s Aerodrome was a full-scale, man-carrying airplane built in 1903. It was an entirely new, powered-flight concept that scaled up his earlier unmanned “Aerodrome” models to sustain heavier-than-air flight for the War Department. The aircraft’s structurally weak, aerodynamically unsound design crashed in both Army-funded launch attempts and was never adopted. Only its engine and partial airframe are at the Smithsonian.
Proposer Details: Langley was known for inventing the sensitive bolometer for infrared research. From his Smithsonian post he secured War-Department funds to pursue powered flight, proposing and building the series of Aerodrome aircraft after earlier success with smaller unmanned models.
